name: GitHub
description: Access repos, issues, PRs, and GitHub Actions workflows

toolSpecs.name: github_list_repositories
toolSpecs.description: List repositories for the connected GitHub account or configured organization.

owner.description: GitHub user or organization login. Defaults to the configured org or authenticated owner.

toolSpecs.name: github_get_file_contents
toolSpecs.description: Fetch file contents from a repository branch, tag, or commit.

path.description: Path to the file within the repository.

ref.description: Optional branch, tag, or commit SHA. Defaults to the repository default branch.

toolSpecs.name: github_list_pull_requests
toolSpecs.description: List pull requests for a repository with optional state filtering.

toolSpecs.name: github_create_issue
toolSpecs.description: Create a GitHub issue in the target repository.

mcp.notes: No first-class Nora MCP bridge is wired for GitHub yet.

usageHints: Use GitHub for repository inspection, code review context, pull request workflows, and issue tracking.
Prefer the configured org and default repo when the operator does not specify a target explicitly.
Inside a running agent, use the generated nora-integrations skill and nora-integration-tool command to execute connected GitHub tools.

name: GitLab
description: Manage GitLab repos, merge requests, pipelines, and issues

steps: Sign in to GitLab and open User Settings → Access Tokens.
Click Add new token, name it (e.g. 'Nora'), pick an expiry, and select scopes.
Copy the generated token (you won't see it again) and paste it into Nora.
For self-hosted GitLab, also fill in your instance URL (e.g. https://gitlab.example.com).

mcp.notes: Reference MCP server. Set GITLAB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN and (optionally) GITLAB_API_URL for self-hosted.

name: Bitbucket
description: Connect to Bitbucket Cloud repos, PRs, and pipelines

steps: Sign in to Bitbucket and open Personal settings → App passwords.
Click Create app password, name it, and select the permissions Nora needs.
Copy the generated app password (you won't see it again) and paste it into Nora.
Enter your Bitbucket username (not your email) so the basic-auth header can be built.

name: Jira
description: Create and manage Jira issues, sprints, and boards

name: Linear
description: Manage Linear issues, projects, and cycles

name: Slack
description: Send messages, receive commands, and integrate with Slack workflows

name: Discord
description: Interact with Discord servers via bots and webhooks

steps: Open the Discord Developer Portal and click New Application.
In the left nav, open Bot, then click Reset Token to generate a bot token (copy it once).
Under OAuth2 → URL Generator, pick the bot scope and the permissions your agent needs, then visit the generated URL to invite the bot to your server.
Right-click your server in Discord → Copy Server ID (enable Developer Mode in User Settings if you don't see it). Paste it into the Server (Guild) ID field.

name: Telegram
description: Connect a Telegram bot for operator messaging and notifications

steps: Open Telegram and start a chat with @BotFather.
Send /newbot, give your bot a name and a username ending in 'bot'.
BotFather replies with an HTTP API token in the form <id>:<secret> — copy it.
(Optional) Send a message to your bot, then call https://api.telegram.org/bot<token>/getUpdates to find your numeric user ID and paste it into the Operator User ID field.

name: Microsoft Teams
description: Send notifications and messages to Microsoft Teams channels

steps: In Teams, go to the channel where messages should appear.
Click ⋯ next to the channel name → Manage channel → Connectors.
Find Incoming Webhook, click Add, give it a name, and click Create.
Copy the generated webhook URL and paste it into Nora.

name: WeCom
description: Configure the official WeCom OpenClaw plugin for bot mode, agent mode, or both from Nora's integrations panel.
usageHints: Bot mode uses a WeCom bot account with WebSocket transport.
Agent mode uses encrypted HTTP callbacks and requires Corp ID, Agent ID, Token, and Encoding AES Key.
Use Additional Accounts JSON only when you need more than the default WeCom account in this first version.

steps: Create or identify the WeCom bot or self-built app you want Nora to configure.
For Bot mode, collect the Bot ID and Secret from the WeCom bot setup flow.
For Agent mode, collect Corp ID, Corp Secret, Agent ID, Token, and Encoding AES Key from the WeCom admin console.
On running OpenClaw agents, Nora will install and configure the official WeCom plugin automatically after you connect.

name: Email (IMAP + SMTP)
description: Connect a mailbox over IMAP and SMTP so the agent can use those credentials in scheduled or interactive workflows.

configFields.label: Cron Prompt
configFields.defaultValue: Look for any new emails or calendar invites I should be aware of and summarize anything important for me.

usageHints: Use Email when an agent needs seamless IMAP and SMTP credentials without Nora shipping opinionated inbox-processing behavior.
Gmail and Outlook presets prefill server settings; Custom exposes the raw host and port fields.
Enable the optional cron if you want Nora to seed a scheduled agent reminder based on this mailbox connection.

name: OpenAI
description: Access GPT models, embeddings, DALL-E, and Whisper APIs

steps: Sign in to OpenAI at platform.openai.com.
Open Settings → API Keys → Create new secret key.
Copy the key (sk-...) — OpenAI shows it only once.
(Optional) Find your org ID under Settings → Organization for cost-attribution / project scoping.

name: Anthropic
description: Access Claude models for text generation and analysis

steps: Sign in at console.anthropic.com.
Open Settings → API keys → Create Key.
Copy the key (sk-ant-...) — it's only shown once.
(Optional) Pin a default Claude model (claude-opus-4-7, claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-haiku-4-5).

name: Hugging Face
description: Deploy and query ML models via Hugging Face Inference API

steps: Sign in to huggingface.co.
Open Settings → Access Tokens → New token.
Pick the role: Read for model inference, Write for pushing to repos.
Copy the token (hf_...).

name: Amazon Web Services
description: Access AWS services — S3, Lambda, EC2, SES, and more

steps: In AWS Console → IAM → Users, create a programmatic-access user.
Attach a least-privilege policy (avoid AdministratorAccess unless your agent really needs it).
Open the user → Security credentials → Create access key → Application running outside AWS.
Copy the Access Key ID + Secret Access Key (only shown once).
Pick a default region (e.g. us-east-1) so the agent doesn't have to specify it on every call.

name: Google Cloud Platform
description: Access GCP services — Compute, Storage, BigQuery, and more

steps: In GCP Console → IAM & Admin → Service Accounts, create a service account.
Grant the roles your agent needs (least privilege wins).
Open the SA → Keys → Add Key → Create new key → JSON.
Paste the entire JSON file contents into the textarea.

name: Microsoft Azure
description: Access Azure services — VMs, Storage, Functions, and more

steps: In the Azure Portal, open Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) → App registrations → New registration.
Once registered, copy the Application (client) ID and Directory (tenant) ID.
Open Certificates & secrets → New client secret. Copy the value (only shown once).
Grant the app the right Azure RBAC roles on the resources / subscription it needs to manage (Subscriptions → Access control → Add role assignment).

name: DigitalOcean
description: Manage DigitalOcean Droplets, Spaces, and Kubernetes clusters

steps: Sign in to cloud.digitalocean.com.
Open API → Tokens/Keys → Generate New Token.
Pick Full Access (read + write) or scope to specific permissions.
Copy the token (only shown once).

name: PostgreSQL
description: Connect to PostgreSQL databases for querying and management

steps: Provision a Postgres user with the privileges your agent needs (avoid using the superuser).
Get the host, port, database name, username, and password from your provider (RDS / Cloud SQL / Supabase / self-hosted).
The Postgres host must be reachable from the agent's network — Nora doesn't tunnel.

mcp.notes: Reads connection details from a single POSTGRES_URL env var.

name: MongoDB
description: Connect to MongoDB for document storage and querying

steps: From MongoDB Atlas (or your self-hosted setup), build a connection string of the form mongodb+srv://<user>:<pass>@<host>/<db>.
URL-encode any special characters in username/password.
Test it locally with mongosh or your driver before pasting.

name: Redis
description: Connect to Redis for caching, pub/sub, and data storage

steps: Get host and port from your Redis provider (Redis Cloud, ElastiCache, Upstash, self-hosted).
If your Redis uses ACL or requires-pass, supply the password.
For Redis Cloud / Upstash, use the rediss:// (TLS) endpoint and treat the AUTH password as the credential.

name: Datadog
description: Send metrics, events, and logs to Datadog for monitoring

steps: Sign in to Datadog and open Organization Settings → API Keys.
Click New Key, name it (e.g. 'Nora'), copy the value.
(Optional) Generate an Application Key under Application Keys for query-time access.
Set Datadog Site if you're not on us1 (e.g. 'datadoghq.eu', 'us3.datadoghq.com').

name: PagerDuty
description: Create incidents and manage on-call schedules in PagerDuty

steps: Sign in to PagerDuty as an admin.
Open Integrations → API Access Keys → Create New API Key.
Pick read-only or read-write scope.
(Optional) For Events API v2, also create a service integration to get a routing key.

name: Notion
description: Create and manage pages, databases, and workspaces in Notion

steps: Open Notion integrations page (notion.so/my-integrations) and click New integration.
Pick the workspace, name your integration, and choose Internal as the type.
Copy the Internal Integration Token (starts with secret_).
From the page or database you want the agent to access, click Share → Add the integration.

mcp.notes: Official Notion MCP server. Reads NOTION_TOKEN env var.

name: Airtable
description: Access and manage Airtable bases, tables, and records

steps: Sign in to Airtable and open the personal access tokens page (airtable.com/create/tokens).
Click Create new token. Pick scopes (data.records:read, data.records:write, schema.bases:read).
Add the bases the agent should access under Access.
Copy the token (starts with pat...).

name: Google Sheets
description: Read from and write to Google Sheets spreadsheets

steps: In GCP Console, create a service account and download a JSON key.
Enable the Google Sheets API for the project (APIs & Services → Library).
Share each spreadsheet you want the agent to access with the SA's client_email.
Paste the JSON contents into the textarea.

name: Trello
description: Manage Trello boards, lists, and cards

steps: Open Trello Power-Ups admin (trello.com/power-ups/admin) and create a new Power-Up — this gives you the API Key.
On the Power-Up's API Key page, click Token (right side) to OAuth-authorize a token.
Copy the API Key + Token (Trello shows the token once after the OAuth flow).
(Optional) Find a board ID from its URL — the second path segment after /b/.

name: Asana
description: Create and manage Asana tasks, projects, and workspaces

steps: Sign in to Asana and open My Apps (app.asana.com/0/my-apps).
Click Create new token, name it, accept the API ToS.
Copy the token (only shown once).
(Optional) Find your workspace GID via GET https://app.asana.com/api/1.0/workspaces.

name: Monday.com
description: Manage Monday.com boards, items, and automations

steps: Sign in to monday.com and open Avatar → Developers → My Access Tokens.
Click Show and copy the Personal API Token, OR generate a new one.
Token gives access to whatever you can see in the UI — for finer scoping, use OAuth apps.

name: ClickUp
description: Manage ClickUp tasks, spaces, and lists

steps: Sign in to ClickUp and open Settings → Apps.
Click Generate next to API Token.
Copy the token (starts with pk_...).
(Optional) Find your workspace ID by viewing any workspace URL — it's the long number after /.

name: Salesforce
description: Access Salesforce CRM — leads, contacts, opportunities, and custom objects

steps: In Salesforce, open Setup → App Manager → New Connected App. Set OAuth scopes (api, refresh_token, full).
Run the OAuth 2.0 Web Server flow against your org and capture the access_token + instance_url from the response.
Paste the access_token and instance_url into Nora.
Tokens expire — for production, store the refresh_token outside Nora and rotate.

name: HubSpot
description: Manage HubSpot contacts, deals, tickets, and marketing campaigns

steps: In HubSpot, open Settings → Integrations → Private Apps.
Click Create a private app, name it, and on the Scopes tab pick the CRM scopes you need.
Click Create app → Show token → Copy.
Token starts with pat-na1- (or similar region prefix).

name: Pipedrive
description: Manage Pipedrive deals, contacts, and sales pipelines

steps: Sign in to Pipedrive and open Settings → Personal preferences → API.
Copy the API Token shown there.
(Optional) Find your company domain (the slug before .pipedrive.com).

name: Zendesk
description: Manage Zendesk tickets, users, and support workflows

steps: In Zendesk Admin Center, open Apps and integrations → Zendesk APIs → Settings.
Enable Token access if it isn't already, then click Add API token.
Copy the token (only shown once).
Subdomain = the slug before .zendesk.com. Email = the agent's account email.

name: Google Drive
description: Access, upload, and manage files in Google Drive

steps: Open Google Cloud Console → IAM & Admin → Service Accounts.
Create a service account (or pick an existing one), then create a JSON key (Keys → Add Key → JSON).
Enable the Drive API for the project (APIs & Services → Library → Google Drive API).
Share the Drive folders the agent should access with the service account's client_email.

mcp.notes: Reads GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS pointing at the SA JSON file.

name: Dropbox
description: Upload, download, and manage files in Dropbox

steps: Open the Dropbox App Console and click Create app.
Choose Scoped access, App folder or Full Dropbox, give it a name.
Under Permissions, tick the scopes your agent needs (files.content.read, files.content.write, etc.) and submit.
On the Settings tab, generate an OAuth 2 Access token (or use a refresh token + auth code flow for production).

name: Amazon S3
description: Manage objects and buckets in Amazon S3

steps: In AWS Console → IAM, create a programmatic-access user (or use an existing one).
Attach a policy that grants the S3 actions your agent needs (e.g. AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess, or a tighter custom policy).
Create an access key for the user and copy the Access Key ID + Secret Access Key.
Enter the default bucket and region your agent should target.

name: Stripe
description: Manage payments, subscriptions, customers, and invoices via Stripe

steps: Sign in to dashboard.stripe.com.
Open Developers → API keys.
Use a Restricted Key for production agents — pick only the resources the agent needs.
Copy the secret key (sk_live_... or sk_test_...).
(Optional) Generate a webhook signing secret under Developers → Webhooks if your agent listens for events.

mcp.notes: Official Stripe MCP server. Reads STRIPE_SECRET_KEY env var.

name: PayPal
description: Process payments and manage PayPal transactions

steps: Sign in to developer.paypal.com.
Open Apps & Credentials → Sandbox or Live tab.
Click Create App, give it a name, choose the right environment.
Copy the Client ID and Client Secret. For testing, use sandbox = true.

name: Twitter / X
description: Post tweets and read account or user tweet data through X API v2

toolSpecs.name: twitter_get_me
toolSpecs.description: Get the connected Twitter/X user profile.

user_fields.description: Comma-separated user fields. Defaults to description, profile_image_url, public_metrics, and verified.

toolSpecs.name: twitter_list_user_tweets
toolSpecs.description: List recent tweets for a user id, username, configured default username, or the connected user.

pagination_token.description: Pagination token from a prior response.

tweet_fields.description: Comma-separated tweet fields. Defaults to author_id, conversation_id, created_at, and public_metrics.

toolSpecs.name: twitter_post_tweet
toolSpecs.description: Post a tweet from the connected Twitter/X user.

reply_to_tweet_id.description: Optional tweet id to reply to.

mcp.notes: No first-class Nora MCP bridge is wired for Twitter/X yet.

usageHints: Use an X app configured with the Nora callback URL, then authorize an X user with tweet.read, tweet.write, users.read, and offline.access scopes; do not paste the app-only Bearer Token from Keys and Tokens.
Prefer the configured default username when the operator does not specify a user.
Inside a running OpenClaw agent, use the generated nora-integrations skill and nora-integration-tool command to execute connected Twitter/X tools.

name: LinkedIn
description: Post updates, read profile/email, and access LinkedIn APIs via OAuth 2.0

toolSpecs.name: linkedin_get_me
toolSpecs.description: Get the connected LinkedIn user's OpenID profile (name, email, sub).

toolSpecs.name: linkedin_post_share
toolSpecs.description: Share a post on LinkedIn from the connected user.

mcp.notes: No first-class Nora MCP bridge is wired for LinkedIn yet.

usageHints: Create a LinkedIn app at https://www.linkedin.com/developers/, add the Nora callback URL as an authorized redirect, then enter the Client ID and Client Secret here. Authorize a LinkedIn user with openid, profile, email, and w_member_social scopes; do not paste a manually-issued access token.
Inside a running OpenClaw agent, use the generated nora-integrations skill and nora-integration-tool command to execute connected LinkedIn tools.

name: Facebook
description: Manage Facebook pages, post updates, and read insights

steps: Open Meta for Developers (developers.facebook.com/apps) and create a Business app.
Add the Facebook Login + Pages product.
Use the Graph API Explorer to mint a long-lived Page Access Token (get a User Access Token first, then exchange).
Find the Page ID via the page's About tab.

name: Instagram Graph
description: Read Instagram account data, insights, comments, and media through Meta Graph APIs

steps: Instagram Graph requires a Meta business app — same flow as Facebook.
Convert your Instagram account to a Business or Creator account.
Connect it to a Facebook Page; copy the Page's Instagram Business Account ID via the Graph API.
Use a long-lived User or Page Access Token with instagram_basic + instagram_content_publish scopes.

name: Google Analytics
description: Read Google Analytics data — pageviews, sessions, conversions

steps: Create a service account in GCP and generate a JSON key.
Enable the Google Analytics Data API for the project.
In Google Analytics Admin → Property Access Management, add the SA's client_email as a Viewer.
Find your GA4 Property ID under Property Settings.

name: Mixpanel
description: Track events, query analytics, and manage user data in Mixpanel

steps: Sign in to Mixpanel and open Project settings.
Copy the Project Token (used for ingestion).
(Optional) For server-side / query API access, create a Service Account under Access security and copy its secret.

name: Segment
description: Send events and manage data pipelines via Segment

steps: Sign in to Segment.
Pick the Source you want to write into (or create a new one).
Copy the Write Key from the source's Settings → API Keys.

name: Elasticsearch
description: Index, search, and analyze data in Elasticsearch clusters

steps: Get the cluster's HTTPS endpoint (Elastic Cloud: Deployments → Endpoints; self-hosted: your nginx/ingress URL).
Create a dedicated user with the right roles (e.g. read for search-only agents, kibana_admin for management).
If your cluster has security disabled (dev), leave username/password blank — the test will hit the node anonymously.

name: Pinecone
description: Store and query vector embeddings in Pinecone

steps: Sign in to Pinecone at app.pinecone.io.
Open API Keys, click Create API Key, copy the value.
Note your environment (us-east-1-aws, gcp-starter, etc.) from the project home page.

name: Weaviate
description: Vector database for AI-powered search and retrieval

steps: Open Weaviate Cloud Console (or your self-hosted instance).
Copy the cluster URL (looks like https://your-cluster.weaviate.network).
If the cluster uses API-key auth, generate one in the cluster settings — leave blank for anonymous clusters.

name: Supabase
description: Access Supabase databases, auth, storage, and edge functions

steps: Open your Supabase project's API settings.
Copy the Project URL (https://<ref>.supabase.co).
Copy the service_role key for full DB access, or the anon key for client-style access — paste whichever fits your agent's blast radius.

mcp.notes: Official-track MCP server for Supabase by the community.

name: Firebase
description: Access Firebase Realtime DB, Firestore, Auth, and Cloud Functions

steps: Open Firebase Console → Project Settings → Service accounts.
Click Generate new private key — Firebase downloads a JSON file.
Paste the entire JSON contents into the Service Account JSON field.
(Realtime Database only) Copy the database URL from the Realtime Database tab.

name: Twilio
description: Send SMS, make calls, and manage WhatsApp via Twilio APIs

steps: Sign in to the Twilio Console.
Copy your Account SID and Auth Token from the dashboard's Account Info panel.
(Optional) Buy or assign a phone number under Phone Numbers → Manage → Active numbers and paste it as the From Number.

name: SendGrid
description: Send transactional and marketing emails via SendGrid

steps: Sign in to SendGrid and open Settings → API Keys.
Click Create API Key, choose Full Access (or Restricted with Mail Send) and copy the key.
(Optional) Verify a sender at Settings → Sender Authentication and paste the email into the From Email field.

name: Terraform
description: Manage infrastructure as code with Terraform Cloud/Enterprise

steps: Sign in to Terraform Cloud and open User settings → Tokens.
Click Create an API token, name it (e.g. 'Nora'), and copy the value.
Paste the token into Nora and (optionally) enter your organization slug.

name: Jenkins
description: Trigger builds, view job status, and manage Jenkins pipelines

steps: Sign in to your Jenkins instance and click your username in the top-right.
Open Configure → API Token → Add new Token.
Copy the generated token and paste it into Nora alongside your Jenkins URL and username.
The Jenkins URL must be reachable from the Nora deployment (RFC1918 / loopback URLs are rejected).

name: CircleCI
description: Trigger pipelines, view build status, and manage CircleCI projects

steps: Sign in to CircleCI at app.circleci.com.
Open User settings → Personal API Tokens.
Click Create New Token, give it a name, and copy the value.
Paste the token into Nora.

name: Vercel
description: Manage Vercel deployments, domains, and environment variables

steps: Sign in to Vercel and open Account Settings → Tokens.
Click Create, name the token, set an expiration, and pick the scope.
If you're managing a team's projects, copy the team ID from Team Settings → General.
Paste the token (and optional team ID) into Nora.

name: Sentry
description: Track errors, performance, and releases in Sentry

steps: Sign in to sentry.io.
Open Settings → Account → API → Auth Tokens.
Click Create New Token. Pick scopes (project:read, project:write, org:read).
Copy the token (only shown once).

name: Grafana
description: Query dashboards, create alerts, and manage Grafana resources

steps: Open your Grafana instance → Administration → Service accounts → Add service account.
Pick a role (Viewer, Editor, Admin) and click Add service account token.
Copy the token.
Set the Grafana URL to your instance origin (must be reachable from Nora; loopback rejected).

name: Zapier
description: Trigger Zapier Zaps and integrate with 5000+ apps

steps: In Zapier, create a new Zap and pick Webhooks by Zapier as the Trigger.
Choose Catch Hook as the trigger event. Zapier generates a unique webhook URL.
Copy the URL into Nora and finish the Zap.

name: Make (Integromat)
description: Trigger Make scenarios and orchestrate complex automations

steps: In Make, create a new Scenario and add a Webhooks → Custom webhook trigger.
Click Add to generate the webhook URL.
Copy the URL into Nora and finish your scenario.

name: n8n
description: Trigger n8n workflows via webhooks for self-hosted automation

steps: In your n8n instance, create a workflow with a Webhook trigger node.
Configure the path and HTTP method, save the workflow, and activate it.
Copy the production webhook URL n8n shows.
(Optional) For n8n Cloud, also paste your API key for management calls.

name: Shopify
description: Manage Shopify products, orders, customers, and inventory

steps: In Shopify Admin, open Settings → Apps and sales channels → Develop apps.
Create a custom app, configure Admin API scopes (read_products, write_orders, etc.).
Click Install app, then copy the Admin API access token (starts with shpat_).
Shop Domain = your store slug (e.g. 'my-shop' for my-shop.myshopify.com).

name: WooCommerce
description: Manage WooCommerce products, orders, and customers via REST API

steps: In WordPress Admin, open WooCommerce → Settings → Advanced → REST API.
Click Add Key. Pick Read/Write permissions and select a user.
Copy the Consumer Key (ck_...) and Consumer Secret (cs_...).
Site URL = your WordPress site origin (e.g. https://shop.example.com).

name: Google Calendar
description: Create, read, and manage Google Calendar events

steps: In GCP Console, create a service account and download a JSON key.
Enable the Google Calendar API for the project.
For each calendar the agent should access, share it with the SA's client_email (in Calendar Settings → Share with specific people).
Paste the JSON contents into the textarea.

name: Confluence
description: Create and manage Confluence pages, spaces, and content

steps: Open id.atlassian.com/manage-profile/security/api-tokens.
Click Create API token, name it (e.g. 'Nora'), copy the value.
Enter your Confluence Cloud URL (e.g. https://acme.atlassian.net) — the same site you sign in to.
Enter the email tied to your Atlassian account.

name: Docker Hub
description: Manage Docker images, repositories, and automated builds

steps: Sign in to Docker Hub at hub.docker.com.
Open Account Settings → Security → Personal access tokens.
Click Generate new token, set the access (Read & Write or Read only), and copy the token.
Paste the token into Nora alongside your Docker Hub username.

name: Kubernetes
description: Manage Kubernetes clusters, deployments, pods, and services

steps: Generate a kubeconfig for your cluster (cloud-provider console, kubeadm, k3s, etc.).
Make sure the context inside the kubeconfig points to the user/SA Nora should authenticate as.
Paste the full kubeconfig YAML into the textarea (it's encrypted at rest).
Optionally pin the active context by name — leave blank to use current-context from the file.

mcp.notes: Community MCP server for Kubernetes. Reads KUBECONFIG from the environment.

name: Algolia
description: Manage search indices and query records in Algolia

steps: Open the Algolia dashboard → API Keys.
Copy the Application ID.
For full management access, use the Admin API Key. For read-only agents, generate a new key with Search-only ACLs and use that instead.
